nativeRange.habitat: Forest streams and blackwater peat swamps; found in both fast-flowing rocky streams and slow tannin-stained waters with leaf litter; spends much of its time near the surface

decorPreferences: open swimming lanes along tank length
dim lighting or floating plants to diffuse light
driftwood and leaf litter for tannin-stained blackwater biotope
fine-leaved plants (Java moss, hornwort) along back and sides

diet.notes: Accepts high-quality flake and small sinking granules as a staple. Condition and colour are significantly improved with regular offerings of live or frozen foods such as bloodworm, daphnia, and brine shrimp. In the wild the species feeds primarily on surface insects and small invertebrates.

avoidWith: nano or slow-moving fish (outcompeted at feeding or stressed by activity)
very small shrimp (fry and juveniles may be eaten)

summary: A large, active schooling rasbora from Southeast Asian forest streams, distinguished by two bold flank spots — one below the dorsal fin and one at the caudal peduncle. Generally peaceful and easy to keep, but needs a long tank and a group of at least six to thrive.
careNotes: The Twospot Rasbora is one of the larger species in the Rasbora genus, typically reaching 12–15 cm in aquaria, with published morphological maxima of up to 20 cm. A minimum tank footprint of 120 cm × 45 cm (approximately 255 litres) is recommended to provide the open swimming lanes this active, fast-moving species requires. It is best kept in groups of at least six; smaller groups result in skittish behaviour and subdued colouration. Slightly acidic, soft water (pH 5.0–7.0, gH 1–10) reflecting its Southeast Asian blackwater and forest-stream origins is ideal, though the species shows good tolerance of neutral water (up to pH 7.5) provided it is clean and stable. Decor should include open midwater swimming space with dim lighting or floating plants; a biotope-style setup with driftwood, leaf litter, and tannin-stained water will elicit the best colouration. The species is an active surface-oriented jumper — a tight-fitting lid is essential. Organic waste accumulation should be avoided with regular water changes; the species is sensitive to deteriorating water quality. Intolerant of copper-based medications at standard doses; use half-dose or alternative treatments if required. Modern molecular taxonomy places Rasbora elegans in the family Danionidae (subfamily Rasborinae) within Cypriniformes, though it is still widely listed under the traditional family Cyprinidae in older references and trade literature.
breedingNotes: An egg-scatterer with no parental care. Breeding is achievable in a separate, shallow, dimly lit spawning tank with fine-leaved plants or a Java moss mat over a mesh or grid bottom to protect eggs from adult predation. Soft, slightly acidic water (pH 6.0–6.5, gH 2–4) and a temperature at the upper end of the range (25–26 °C) are conducive to spawning. A conditioning period of 1–2 weeks with generous live and frozen food prior to introduction of the breeding pair is recommended. Adults will readily consume eggs and fry — remove parents immediately after spawning is observed. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours; fry are free-swimming within 2–3 days and accept infusoria or commercial liquid fry food initially, progressing to micro-worm and baby brine shrimp as they grow.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Rasbora elegans
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size (up to 15 cm typical, 20 cm morphological max), temperature 22–25 °C, pH 5.0–7.5, hardness 2–12 °dH, native range (Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Borneo, Sumatra), habitat (forest streams and peat swamps), minimum tank 120 cm × 45 cm × 45 cm / 255 L, schooling requirement (min 6), diet guidance, breeding method, and the escape-risk/tight-lid advisory.

additional.name: FishBase — Rasbora elegans
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (20 cm SL), temperature (22–25 °C), pH (6.0–6.5), hardness (to dH 10), native range (Malay Peninsula, Singapore, Borneo, Sumatra), habitat (forest streams), taxonomy (Danionidae / Cypriniformes), diet classified as carnivorous (trophic level 3.3). IUCN Least Concern (assessed April 2020).

additional.name: Wikipedia — Twospot rasbora
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Danionidae, Cypriniformes, described Volz 1903), native range detail (Peninsular Malaysia including Johor/Pahang/Perak/Kelantan, Borneo, Sumatra/Nias, Singapore), physical description of the two distinct flank spots (rectangular spot below dorsal fin origin and triangular spot at caudal peduncle), and surface-feeding behaviour.

additional.name: Aqua-Fish.net — Twospot Rasbora Care Guide
additional.notes: Supplemental trade source. Provided typical aquarium size (12–16 cm), temperature (22–26 °C), pH (6.3–7.0), hardness (2–10 °dH), tank length requirement (120 cm), lifespan estimate (5–6 years), swim zone (mid), compatibility guidance, and egg-scatterer breeding notes including 24–48 hour hatch time.

additional.name: Ecology Asia — Two-spot Rasbora
additional.notes: Regional field reference for Southeast Asian distribution; confirmed presence across Peninsular Malaysia, Singapore, Borneo, and Sumatra in forest stream and peat swamp habitats.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; requires a separate spawning tank with fine-leaved plants or Java moss over mesh, soft acidic water, and conditioning on live foods. Adults consume eggs — remove immediately after spawning. Eggs hatch in 24–48 hours.
